original_hypothesis.title: MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION Constant Defined but Never Enforced in placeOrder
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The constant MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION = 1e16 (1% from parity) is defined to restrict stablecoin order prices near 1:1, but placeOrder() never references it. Any non-zero price is accepted, including extreme values far from parity. This enables deceptive orders at prices that exploit automated taker bots or confused users, and is a prerequisite for the uint128 truncation scenario in fillOrder.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect: the developers explicitly defined a constant intended to protect price sanity, but the enforcement code was never written. Orders at price=1 (effectively free) or price=uint128.max (exploiting downstream math) are accepted. For a stablecoin DEX, allowing arbitrary pricing defeats the core design purpose.

proof_of_signal.pattern: MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION defined at line 46-47 as uint128 public constant = 1e16; placeOrder validates price != 0 at line 189 but never checks |price - 1e18| <= MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION

preconditions: None—any user can place orders at any price

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: placeOrder only checks: if (price == 0) revert InvalidPrice(); Missing: if (abs(price - 1e18) > MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION) revert InvalidPrice();
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Place order with price=1 (1 wei per token) → taker bots fill at extremely favorable rates; or price=uint128.max to set up truncation conditions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Deceptive pricing enables value extraction from automated takers or users who don't verify order prices

economic_questions: Are automated taker bots expected to interact with this orderbook?
What price validation do downstream consumers (MPP routing) perform?
validation_requirements: Confirm placeOrder accepts price values far from 1e18
Test order placement at extreme prices (1, uint128.max)
Verify MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION is unreferenced outside its declaration

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Requires deposit of base tokens to place order
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on whether automated bots fill extreme-priced orders
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable—up to near-total if bot fills at price=1
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction to place; profit on fill

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When automated taker bots are active
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Orderbook pollution, loss of pricing integrity

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Automated takers/bots that don't verify prices, confused users
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% of fill amount at extreme prices

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION = 1e16 (1%), never referenced in validation logic
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Search entire contract for any use of MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION beyond declaration

validation_result.impact_summary: MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION constant is defined but never enforced in placeOrder(), allowing orders at any non-zero price. This defeats stablecoin parity protection and is a prerequisite for the uint128 truncation vulnerability in fillOrder().
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The constant MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION = 1e16 (1% from parity) is explicitly defined at line 47 of StablecoinDEX.sol with the comment '1% from parity', establishing clear developer intent to restrict order prices near the 1:1 stablecoin peg. However, the placeOrder() function at lines 178-218 only checks `if (price == 0) revert InvalidPrice()` at line 189. There is no validation comparing the price to MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION anywhere in the contract. A grep of the entire source reveals MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION appears only in its declaration (line 47) and nowhere else. This means any non-zero uint128 price is accepted, from 1 wei to type(uint128).max (~3.4e38). For a stablecoin DEX designed to operate near 1:1 parity, this is a clear code defect. The missing enforcement also directly enables the uint128 truncation exploit in fillOrder (INV-STATE-001): an attacker can place an order at an extreme price, and when filled, the quoteAmount calculation overflows uint128 and is silently truncated.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Line 47 defines the constant: `uint128 public constant MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION = 1e16;`. Lines 184-189 show the complete price validation in placeOrder: only `if (price == 0) revert InvalidPrice();`. No other function in the contract references MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION. The constant is dead code — defined but never used.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet: uint128 public constant MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION = 1e16; // 1% from parity
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This constant is defined to enforce price bounds near 1:1 parity for stablecoin pairs but is never referenced in any validation logic anywhere in the contract.

The only price validation is `price == 0`. There is no check such as `if (price < 1e18 - MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION || price > 1e18 + MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION) revert InvalidPrice();`. Any non-zero uint128 value is accepted as a valid price, including 1 (virtually free) or type(uint128).max (~3.4e38).

calling_sequence: 1. User calls placeOrder(baseToken, quoteToken, price=type(uint128).max, amount=MIN_ORDER_SIZE)
2. Line 184-185: Zero address checks pass (valid token addresses)
5. Line 190-191: Balance check passes (user has sufficient balance)
6. Lines 194-207: Order is created and inserted into linked list with extreme price
7. No MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION check exists anywhere in the path
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The placeOrder function performs four validations (lines 184-191): zero address check, minimum order size, non-zero price, and balance sufficiency. The price validation at line 189 only checks `price == 0`. The MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION constant at line 47 is never referenced. An order with price=1 (1 wei per token, effectively free) or price=type(uint128).max passes all checks. This directly enables exploitation: (1) deceptive orders at extreme prices for social engineering, and (2) the uint128 truncation vulnerability in fillOrder when quoteAmount = (amount * extreme_price) / 1e18 exceeds type(uint128).max.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The foundry.toml only configures compiler settings. The constant is hardcoded in the contract source.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is straightforward and requires no special conditions: (1) Attacker deposits baseToken via deposit(). (2) Attacker calls placeOrder(baseToken, quoteToken, type(uint128).max, amount). The extreme price passes validation since it's non-zero. (3) The order sits in the orderbook at a price of ~3.4e38 (vs expected ~1e18 for stablecoin parity). (4) If any taker (automated bot or confused user) fills this order, they would pay an astronomically inflated quoteAmount — or if combined with the uint128 truncation bug, the taker pays a truncated (tiny) amount while receiving full baseTokens, stealing from the maker. Either way, the missing validation enables a class of attacks that the constant was designed to prevent.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity as a standalone issue (SWC-135 dead code / missing validation). The direct impact is orderbook pollution with extreme-priced orders that violate the stablecoin DEX's core design premise of near-parity trading. However, the severity escalates when combined with the uint128 truncation in fillOrder (INV-STATE-001), as MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION enforcement would have prevented the extreme prices needed to trigger quoteAmount overflow. The missing check defeats a deliberate safety mechanism the developers defined.
recommended_mitigations: Add price deviation validation in placeOrder: require(price >= 1e18 - MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION && price <= 1e18 + MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION, 'Price out of range');
Consider making MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION configurable per token pair to accommodate different stablecoin pairs with varying typical spreads
Add SafeCast or explicit overflow check for the uint128 downcast in fillOrder as defense-in-depth
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy StablecoinDEX and two mock ERC20 tokens. 2. Deposit baseToken for test user. 3. Call placeOrder with price=1 — confirm it succeeds (should have been rejected). 4. Call placeOrder with price=type(uint128).max — confirm it succeeds. 5. Verify MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION is never referenced by searching contract bytecode or running `grep -n MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION` on source.
uncertainties: Whether automated taker bots or MPP routing actually interact with this orderbook and would fill extreme-priced orders without their own price validation
Whether there is off-chain infrastructure that validates prices before submitting fill transactions
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test placeOrder with random price values to confirm all non-zero values are accepted
Integration test combining extreme price order placement with fillOrder to verify the uint128 truncation path
Search for any external or off-chain price validation that might mitigate the on-chain gap
